sanskrit_devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
अक्षरं ब्रह्म परमं स्वभावोऽध्यात्ममुच्यते ।
भूतभावोद्भवकरो विसर्गः कर्मसंज्ञितः ॥ ३ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca
akṣaraṁ brahma paramaṁ svabhāvo 'dhyātmam ucyate |
bhūta-bhāvodbhava-karo visargaḥ karma-saṁjñitaḥ || 3 ||

anvaya.meaning: adhyātma — the principle pertaining to the self
is called / is said to be

translations.verbatim_quote: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: The indestructible, transcendental living entity is called Brahman, and his eternal nature is called adhyātma, the self. Action pertaining to the development of the material bodies of the living entities is called karma, or fruitive activities.

The Supreme Lord said: The Supreme indestructible Reality is called Brahman; one's own self is called adhyatma. Actions pertaining to the material personality of living beings, and its development is called karma, or fruitive activities.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Brahman is indestructible, eternal, and its nature is never changed at any time. But beyond Brahman there is Parabrahman. Brahman refers to the living entity, and Parabrahman refers to the Supreme Personality of Godhead.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 8.3 Aksharam, the Imperishable, the indestructible, is the param brahma, supreme Brahman. The svabhavah, nature, of that very supreme Brahman, the Self of all beings — that nature, the Self existing inwardly in each individual, is ucyate, called; adhyatmam, adhyatma — the very 'I' that begins the process of [each individual's] empirical activity.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads akṣara brahma as the supreme nondual Brahman, the Imperishable. svabhāva (one's own nature) is the principle by which that supreme Brahman exists inwardly as the Self in each individual — that is adhyātma. The 'visarga' that originates beings is karma, identified with the ritual offering whose result is bhūta-bhāva-udbhava (the arising of beings' existence). Three of the seven questions are answered in this single verse with maximum doctrinal precision.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 8.3 The supreme imperishable is the individual self — supreme because it is higher than non-conscious matter. Adhyatma — that which has been pointed out as relating to the self — is svabhava — that which abides as the very nature of each individual self. Karma is the visarga — the offering of substances etc., which causes the origination of all beings.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads akṣara brahma here as the individual self (jīvātman), supreme over non-conscious matter — a Vishishtadvaita-specific reading that diverges sharply from Shankara's Advaita reading of akṣara brahma as the supreme nondual Brahman. svabhāva is the inherent nature of each individual self; adhyātma is what relates to that self. karma is the ritual offering (visarga) that originates beings. The traditions diverge on the metaphysics of akṣara brahma but agree on the structural pattern: three of the seven questions answered crisply in one verse.

positions.rendering: the indestructible, transcendental living entity is called Brahman (the jīva, with Parabrahman beyond)
the Supreme indestructible Reality is called Brahman
the Imperishable supreme Brahman, the Self of all beings (Advaita: nondual Brahman)
the supreme imperishable is the individual self, higher than non-conscious matter (Vishishtadvaita)

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: akṣara brahma (the imperishable Brahman) is the verse's metaphysical apex. Advaita reads it as the supreme nondual Brahman; Vishishtadvaita reads it as the jīvātman (individual self) higher than matter; Gaudiya reads the akṣara as the jīva with Parabrahman beyond it. The three traditions diverge maximally on the metaphysics — this is one of the most contested compounds in the chapter. The source pack records all three traditional readings explicitly. The engineering analog cannot translate this; honest scope-acknowledgement is required.

positions.rendering: action pertaining to the development of material bodies is called karma
actions pertaining to material personality and its development is karma
the visarga (creative offering) that originates beings is karma — identified with ritual offering
karma is the visarga, the offering of substances etc., which causes the origination of all beings

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: visarga is technically the 'sending forth' or 'creative discharge'. Shankara and Ramanuja both read it as the ritual offering (yajña) whose result is the origination of beings — connecting karma in this verse explicitly back to the yajña-cycle of chapter 3 (3.10-3.16). Modern translations (Prabhupada, Mukundananda) generalize to 'action pertaining to material bodies' and lose the ritual-specific force. The engineering layer should reference chapter 3's yajña-cycle reading and preserve the Sanskrit visarga at source-pack level.

literal_meaning: The Blessed Lord said: The imperishable supreme is Brahman. One's own nature is called adhyātma. The offering causing the origination of beings' existence is called karma.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna answers the first three of Arjuna's seven questions in a single verse. (1) brahma = akṣaraṁ paramam, the imperishable supreme. (2) adhyātma = svabhāva, one's own nature / the Self existing inwardly. (3) karma = visarga, the offering that causes beings to originate. The traditions diverge sharply on the metaphysics of akṣara brahma — Advaita reads it as the nondual supreme; Vishishtadvaita reads it as the jīvātman; Gaudiya reads it as the jīva with Parabrahman beyond. All traditions agree on the structural pattern: three crisp definitions in one verse, each preserving a Sanskrit technical term that resists English flattening. The engineering layer is forced to STRETCHED here — the three terms are doctrinally specific.
